data.title: RH: A Comfortable Prospect On A Turnaround
data.description:
I have a good track record in investing, but nobody has a perfect track record. Every investor, if they are in the game long enough, makes calls that don't work out. A good example of one of mine that fits this description would be RH (NYSE:RH). The business, which operates in the luxury retail home furnishings market, has seen a meaningful decline in share price, with the stock falling 34.3% since I rated it a ‘buy’ in March of 2022. This is at a time when the S&P 500 is up 41.2%. As disappointing as the returns have been, I do believe that the situation is improving. To be clear, there are headwinds for the company moving forward. But based on recent financial performance and how the stock is valued on both an absolute basis and relative to other similar companies, I do believe that it justifies a soft ‘buy’ rating at this time.

This play makes sense this time

Operationally speaking, RH serves as a retailer and luxury lifestyle brand business that caters to the home furnishings market. It considers itself a fully integrated player in this space, with sales generated through its galleries, which serve as showrooms for its brand, as well as through its websites and other sources. The firm operates retail locations throughout the US, Canada, the UK, Germany, Belgium, and Spain. And it even has several Design Gallery locations that operate as large format stores that include in them, among other things, on-site restaurants, rooftop cafes, and wine bars.

Over the last few years now, RH has experienced a bumpy ride. From 2022 to 2023, for instance, revenue for the business plummeted from $3.59 billion to $3.03 billion. This was in spite of the fact that the number of retail locations had increased from 67 to 70 while the number of outlets grew from 37 to 42. This drop in revenue was caused by a return to more normal spending patterns after the COVID-19 pandemic caused a surge in home spending. During that time, government stimulus made spending easier, plus individuals were spending a lot more time at home because of social distancing. That caused major investments to be made in the types of goods that RH offers. In addition to this return to more normal levels, the company also saw pain because of high interest rates that made costly purchases prohibitive.

While that drop in revenue was painful, it is important to point out that, in 2024, revenue staged a slight recovery, growing to $3.18 billion. This was caused by the introduction of new collections that appealed to customers, a surge in catalog circulation, and increased hospitality revenue thanks to new gallery openings. Outlet revenue for the company also improved during this time. The year-over-year increase for the business would have been even better had it not been for the fact that, in 2023, the company had an extra operating week that added $50 million in revenue during that year.

Even though revenue has started to recover, as of 2024, the same cannot be said of profits and cash flows. From 2022 to 2023, net income for the company plummeted from $528.6 million to $127.6 million. There was a further drop to $72.4 million last year. This was in spite of the fact that, from 2023 to 2024, the overall gross profit for the company grew from $1.39 billion to $1.41 billion. However, selling, general, and administrative costs popped from $1.02 billion to $1.09 billion. Interest expense grew also, climbing from $198.3 million to $230.6 million. Other profitability metrics worsened during this time. Operating cash flow was only $17.1 million last year. That was down from the $202.2 million the previous year and the $403.7 million a year before that. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a decline over the last three years from $902.1 million to $431.6 million. And finally, EBITDA for the business contracted from $930.5 million to $539.1 million.

One thing that I am encouraged is that, so far, 2025 is looking up. We only have data for the first quarter of the year. But during that time, revenue of $814 million easily surpassed the $727 million reported last year. This increase in sales for the business was caused by core operations and management attributed to continued product transformation and platform changes. The firm also benefited from new gallery openings and an increase in outlet revenue.

Profitability for the company is also getting better. The firm went from a net loss of $3.6 million in the first quarter of 2024 to a profit of $8 million the same time this year. Operating cash flow jumped from $56.1 million to $86.6 million. And on an adjusted basis, the increase was from $74.2 million to $88.7 million. Lastly, EBITDA for the company grew from $89.1 million to $106.4 million.

Management has been working hard to fundamentally transform the company. For this year as a whole, the firm is forecasting revenue growth of between 10% and 13%. International momentum has been particularly appealing, with gallery revenue for its RH England operations being up 47% in the first quarter and online demand rising by 44%. In RH Munich and RH Dusseldorf, demand seems to be up around 60%. The firm is also working on opening RH Paris, with management even describing it as ‘our most elegant and inspiring gallery yet.’ Unless something changes in the timeline, that location should open early next month. Management continues to launch new brand extensions. But a significant one that was originally planned for the second half of this year has now been pushed to the spring of 2026 in order for the company to mitigate the impact of tariffs.

Speaking of tariffs, management has been a bit cautious. But the good news is that the firm should be able to adjust to the new economic order. In the first quarter of this year, 16% of what the business purchased was sourced out of China. By the end of this fiscal year, that should drop to roughly 2%. By the end of 2025, management believes that around 52% of upholstered furniture will be produced in the US, with another 21% being produced in Italy. These challenges that the company is facing have not prevented it from forecasting an EBITDA margin of 20% to 21% this year. At the midpoint with the expected revenue growth as well, this would mean EBITDA of $727.1 million based on revenue of $3.55 billion. This would likely translate to adjusted operating cash flow of around $582.1 million.

If we use these estimates provided by management, we can see in the chart above how shares are currently priced. These multiples are at the high end of what I would consider to be the fair value range. And relative to other similar firms, shares are a bit on the cheap side. In the table below, I compared RH to five other enterprises. What I found was that only one of these companies was cheaper than it is on a price-to-operating cash flow basis. And when it came to the EV-to-EBITDA approach, only two of the five were cheaper.

Company Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
RH 7.6 10.4
Arhaus (ARHS) 12.4 5.7
Beyond (BYON) 54.6 28.8
Haverty Furniture Companies (HVT) 7.0 10.1
Sleep Number Corporation (SNBR) 82.8 13.5
Hooker Furnishings (HOFT) 19.0 21.2

Takeaway

At this point in time, I definitely understand that I overestimated the company and its potential a couple of years ago. Now, I am more measured. Having said that, it is clear that the company is recovering nicely. 2025 should be a solid year for the business, and it's likely that future years will be positive as well. This is especially the case thanks to its international expansion efforts. Add on top of this how the stock is currently priced and the firm's tariff mitigation strategy, and I do believe that rating it a soft ‘buy’ would be appropriate at this time.

data.title: John Hancock Multimanager 2015 Lifetime Portfolio Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Highlights

  • After a tumultuous start to the second quarter, the financial markets regained their footing and provided positive total returns for the full three months.
  • The portfolio, while also delivering a gain, didn't keep pace with its benchmark.
  • Asset allocation and underlying manager results each weighed on relative performance.

Market review and outlook

The world equity markets rebounded from initial weakness to register strong gains in the second quarter. Stocks fell sharply in early April in response to U.S. tariff announcements on concerns that Washington's new approach to trade would lead to slower economic growth. Within a week, however, the U.S. administration reversed course and declared a 90-day pause on tariffs. The move fueled hopes that the initial plan was largely a negotiating tactic, providing investors with confidence that trade policy would ultimately be less restrictive than first thought. In addition, data related to global growth, inflation, and corporate earnings exhibited stability despite the shifting policy backdrop. Equities recovered all of their earlier losses in the ensuing rally, propelling the major world indexes to all-time highs by the end of June. Growth stocks, lowerquality companies, and the mega-cap U.S. technology sector led the way higher, while the value style and defensive investments generally underperformed. This marked a pronounced reversal from the trends in place during the first quarter.

Bonds also delivered positive total returns. After experiencing volatility early in the quarter, the fixed-income markets recovered on the strength of low inflation, moderate growth, and expectations that central banks would continue to ease monetary policy. Credit-oriented market segments performed well in this environment and generally outpaced government debt.

Contributors and detractors

Asset allocation detracted from results, as our diversified approach was out of step with the aggressive rally. The real assets portfolio was the primary detractor due to negative returns for energy stocks and U.S. real estate investment trusts. An overweight position in defensive equities—while positive in absolute termsalso weighed on relative performance at a time of heightened investor risk appetites. Allocation in the fixed-income portfolio was a small contributor, primarily as a result of an overweight the emerging markets. The fund also benefited from having an overweight in stocks relative to bonds. On the other hand, the fund's absolute return portfolio detracted. Underlying manager performance also detracted somewhat, with nearly all of the shortfall occurring in the bond portfolio.

Portfolio changes

The first half of the year was characterized by dramatic swings in the markets, both in terms of price movements and investors' rapid shifts among specific asset classes. For our part, we don't attempt to time the market or chase momentum in certain areas that happen to be outperforming in a given month. Instead, we remain focused on the core tenets of our investment process: fundamentals, valuations, and diversification. While we frequently make small changes to the portfolio, the moves are based on rigorous analysis and a three- to five-year outlook. In addition, we strive to keep the portfolio positioned for a broad range of outcomes instead of trying to predict the path of the economy or the direction of government policy. We believe this steady approach, rather than one that attempts to react to the ever-changing daily headlines, is the optimal way to help the fund achieve its longer-term investment objectives.

David Kobuszewski, CFA | Geoffrey Kelley, CFA | Nathan W. Thooft, CFA | Robert E. Sykes, CFA

data.title: StepStone Group: The Middle Man Play On Private Equity
data.description:
Within the alternative asset solutions space, StepStone Group (NASDAQ:STEP) stands out for having one of the largest global networks spanning both the limited partner (e.g., pension funds, endowments, etc.) and general partner (e.g., fund managers) sides. This network is underpinned by dedicated coverage analysts who track managers and maintain proprietary research databases, as well as managers that help clients customize portfolios across private equity and debt, infrastructure, as well as real assets.

In line with industry comparables Hamilton Lane (HLNE) (see Hamilton Lane: A Unique Play On The Rise Of Private Equity) and GCM Grosvenor (GCMG) (see GCM Grosvenor: Riding The Private Equity Surge), STEP makes its money by leveraging its solutions capabilities into various ‘fund of funds’ strategies. The company implements this firstly via separately managed accounts, where STEP tailors a multi-strategy portfolio around a specific investor. Secondly, via commingled funds focused on a specific strategy; think primaries (i.e., investing directly in funds), secondaries (i.e., buying up discounted stakes in existing funds), and co-investments (i.e., investing into companies alongside other sponsors).

Ultimately, the case to own STEP rests on its unique competitive position in a market with secular growth potential. Thanks to private markets’ outperformance and diversification benefits vs public markets, as well as its extended runway now that retail has gained access to private markets (a >$12tn opportunity), the low-teens% market growth we’ve seen likely isn’t slowing down.

Against this backdrop, STEP stands out - not only for its strong fundamentals but also for its stock price, which has underperformed to-date and remains off pre "Liberation Day" highs.

Look Through the Headline ‘Miss’

STEP kicked off its fiscal year 2026 with a mostly in-line quarter. At the top, headline fee revenues came in at $212.7m (+19% YoY), helped by a +17% YoY rise in all-important management fees. Digging deeper, it was SMA revenues that drove the acceleration at +23% YoY, though fee revenue from commingled funds was also very solid at +15% YoY.

One thing to note when interpreting STEP’s headline numbers is the role of non-recurring “retroactive fees” (i.e., performance-related incentive fees from investors coming in after the initial close of a fund). These were much lower this quarter ($2.9m) than last ($19.1m) and, along with performance fees, drove STEP’s miss vs Street estimates. Normalizing for these one-offs, the company’s ‘core’ fee-related earning margin actually expanded by an impressive “more than 300 basis points from a year ago”.

Watch for the Retail Growth Momentum

Underpinning STEP’s long-term growth trajectory is private wealth, which saw consecutive quarters of “more than $1 billion in subscriptions” - despite “Liberation Day” induced volatility earlier in the quarter. As a result, assets under management (or AUM) for the platform were up to “nearly $10 billion” at quarter-end (up from $8.2bn) before it “crossed the $10 billion threshold in July”.

From here, attracting retail flows is a massive opportunity for STEP. Firstly, for the industry-wide growth runway. Consider that private wealth allocation to private assets is currently up to 20 percentage points below institutions. Add to that defined contribution plans (worth $10-15tn), where access is opening up from a near-zero base, and you have a compelling multi-year (if not multi-decade) growth runway for the entire industry.

Secondly, STEP is ahead of its peers in the race for retail money. Note that, unlike institutions, where the default products are drawdown funds that require capital to be committed upfront before getting ‘called’ later on and locked up for years, serving retail requires a much different fund structure. Namely, indefinite-life “evergreen” funds that immediately deploy capital and come with more redemption flexibility. Given that STEP has built a track record here, along with the likes of HLNE and Partners Group (OTCPK:PGPHF) (see Partners Group Is Built Different), it is a frontrunner in the upcoming retail race.

Also Watch for the Retail-Driven Margin Uplift

Besides top-line growth, STEP’s growing shift from institutional to retail also means better fee rates. Note that since launching its evergreen platform and then expanding its distribution footprint over the years (currently “over 550 individual distribution partners”), the blended management fee rate is up to 64bps (vs 52bps in FY21). Commingled funds (where the evergreen funds sit) drove that delta entirely, as fee rates here expanded ~12bps over the last five years to 1.02%.

It remains early days in the retail push, so until competition ramps up, I’d expect more room for commingled fee rate expansion from here. Higher-fee retail products are also poised to grow as a % share of the overall business, from broader industry tailwinds, new product launches, and also from STEP’s distribution (>550 partners, of which “50% are selling more than one evergreen product”). Thus, STEP should see the overall FRE margin expand in tandem.

Upside in the Fundraising Pipeline

As for the shorter term, fundraising is key—after all, the bigger the AUM, the bigger STEP’s earnings power. As of its latest quarter, fundraises appear to be going well. Notable adds include >$400m in corporate direct lending, ~$200m in infrastructure secondaries, a ~$100m “cleanup close” in real estate secondaries (following the $1.2bn raised last quarter), and >$400m in its multi-strategy global venture fund.

Specifically, management disclosed $8.7bn of gross AUM additions thanks to “strong inflows in managed accounts and balanced contributions across commingled funds in all asset classes.” Of that, fee-earning AUM was up by ~$6bn, split 60/40 across SMAs, and commingled funds. By asset class, the fee-earning AUM split was 52/48 across private equity and real estate/infrastructure/private debt.

Looking ahead, there are still quite a few drawdown funds “in market,” many of which are heading for first close in the coming quarters. Notable pipeline callouts from the call include an initial close for the private equity co-investment fund (FQ2 2026) and venture capital AUM (activated in July) becoming fee-earning next quarter. Fundraising for private equity secondaries, potentially coming to market soon, will also be worth monitoring.

Outside of venture capital, performance has been quite solid across the board. Take the private equity flagships, for instance, with IRRs for primaries at +14.1%, secondaries at +14.8%, and co-investments at +15.3%. Given this, along with momentum on the retail side, where STEP continues to innovate with its fund structures, I'd expect more upside than downside risk to future fundraising updates.

Summing Up

Net-net, STEP has a lot going for it at this point. From growth, helped by industry and company-level tailwinds, to profitability, underpinned by its stable management fee base vs. more volatile performance fees for operators like TPG (TPG) (see TPG: Own This Year's Private Equity Laggard Into Earnings) and KKR (KKR), STEP should quite easily grow into its ~25x earnings multiple over time. With the key downside risk, taking out residual non-controlling interests, also being actively addressed by management, I quite like the risk/reward here.

data.title: Madison Square Garden Entertainment: Upgrade To Buy On Better Growth Clarity
data.description:
Summary

I downgraded to a hold rating for Madison Square Garden Entertainment (NYSE:MSGE) previously, as I was worried about the gap left behind by Billy Joel's residency absence and also the FY26 demand outlook. With a much better forward outlook, I upgrade my rating to buy. Management has noted strong bookings for FY26, disclosed plans for a new residency, and highlighted resilient consumer spending trends. Viewed together, I believe these factors have provided far greater clarity on MSGE's growth trajectory and support an upgrade in my rating.

Earnings results update

MSGE 4Q25 results were not pretty. Total revenues declined 17% y/y to $154.1 million, with the bulk of weakness tied to fewer concerts at Madison Square Garden Arena. Even though MSGE did host more shows at its theaters, it just wasn't enough to offset the decline at the flagship venue. Ancillary revenue streams saw the same trend. Food, beverage, and merchandise sales fell 24% y/y to $26.4 million due to fewer Knicks and Rangers games and the reduced concert slate at The Garden. This top-line weakness was coupled with rising costs. SG&A expenses increased 7% y/y to $59.9 million, which, together with weaker sales, drove MSGE to an adj. operating loss of $1.3 million. This was a steep decline from the $13.1 million adj. EBIT in 4Q24.

FY26 Bookings Rebound Addresses Key Overhang

Previously, the main reason I downgraded MSGE was because of the visible near-term weakness in concert bookings. Very encouragingly, the 4Q25 results have directly addressed that concern. Management now expects total events to grow y/y in FY26, driven by a rebound in concerts, including a return to growth at The Garden itself. If this was just management's words, I would be skeptical, but no, this guide is being backed by tangible pacing data. In the earnings call, management noted that MSGE has already secured 80% of its 2026 bookings goal at The Garden and about two-thirds across its theater portfolio. Relative to 2025, both metrics are running ahead. This is a very important proof point because it signals that demand is recovering rapidly. Early signs also point to a particularly strong start to FY26, with 1Q26 set to mark a record number of concerts in a single quarter at The Garden. This inflection matters because concerts at Madison Square Garden are MSGE's highest-margin driver. A healthier event cadence here not only supports revenue recovery but also drives operating leverage.

From a stock sentiment perspective, this is a very positive development, as it shifts the narrative from supply constraints to solid demand recovery and is one that provides MSGE with clearer visibility into revenue and margin acceleration in FY26. This should ease market concerns and set the stage for a positive re-rating in the stock.

A New Residency

If you read my last update on MSGE, one of the key reasons behind my hold rating was the uncertainty over how MSGE would "fill the hole" left by the Billy Joel residency. For those that haven't followed the MSGE story, the Billy Joel residency was a major part of MSGE, one that contributed 11 high-margin concerts in FY24, and its conclusion created a sharp headwind for FY25. The good news is that MSGE has found a solution to fill this void. While the exact details are unknown, it was comforting to hear management disclosed they are in the "late planning stages for a residency next calendar year," which would feature a "substantial number of dates at the arena." This is a very significant development. A major residency does more than just backfill lost shows; it also provides a predictable, high-margin revenue stream that de-risks 2026 P&L.

Also, while the financial benefit of the new residency will not materialize until FY27, I think it's fine because we know that the FY26 concert slate is inflecting higher. This just means there is a visible line of sight to MSGE growth through FY27.

Resilient Consumer Demand and Christmas Spectacular Strength

The last point to note in this update that pushes me to upgrade my rating this round is that underlying consumer demand is a lot stronger than I thought. There are various indicators that point to this. Firstly, in 4Q25, the majority of concerts at MSGE venues sold out; secondly, early indicators for 1Q26 show sell-through rates already pacing ahead of last year; and lastly, this booking momentum is being matched by robust ancillary spending. Put simply, consumers are spending more on events and more on-site. On the latter, it looks even more impressive if you consider that food and beverage per capita spending at The Garden rose by double digits in 4Q25, with the trend accelerating into July. This shows that attendees are not only attending but also spending aggressively on high-margin offerings. The best example of this dynamic is MSGE's wholly owned Christmas Spectacular. FY25 was a record year, generating more than $170 million in revenue from 1.1 million tickets sold. And this momentum continued into FY26. Specifically, the outlook for FY26 is even stronger, with 211 shows scheduled (up from 200) and advanced ticket revenue pacing well ahead of last year, driven by higher yields and increased volume.

Valuation

Relative to my update in February, the growth visibility has improved tremendously, so much so that I believe my previous downgrade is no longer justified. It is clear that FY26 bookings are strong, and even through FY27 there is a new residency that can fill the gap left by Billy Joel. At the same time, consumers are spending more on bookings and even high-margin offerings. Putting all of these together, I don't think it's hard to imagine achieving the consensus FY28 adj. EBITDA target of $252.5 million (implies ~10% CAGR). As a note, historical adj. EBITDA growth (from FY23 to FY25) was also 10%/year. My variant view here is that MSGE should trade higher than 13x forward EBITDA because the growth visibility is a lot better today. I believe 15x is more justifiable since this is the historical average, and at 15x, this implies a share price target of ~$55.

Investment Risk

MSGE's growth outlook is ultimately tied to consumer spending health, and as such, if there is a macro downturn, this will directly hurt its P&L as consumers attend fewer events. Also, while the FY26 outlook is improving, MSGE is still exposed to the inherent volatility of the concert touring cycle. If there is an abrupt change in the tour schedule, it would impact FY26 performance. MSGE also has a high dependency on the continued popularity of the Christmas Spectacular production. Should consumer preference change, it could cause y/y performance to look bad because of tough y/y comps.

Conclusion

In my view, the investment case for MSGE has materially improved. The FY26 event calendar is recovering faster than expected, a new residency provides long-term visibility, and consumer spending strength is a lot better than I expected. At 15x forward EBITDA, which is in line with historical averages, I have a share price target of ~$55/share using consensus FY28 adj. EBITDA estimates. I am upgrading MSGE to a buy rating.

data.title: Planet Labs: Fast Iteration From Agile Aerospace Helps Cash Flow Generation
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Planet Labs PBC (NYSE:PL) recently achieved the milestone of positive operating cash flow despite the relentless increase of CapEx, which also brought a large improvement in its free cash flow. With a lean and mean technological platform for satellite management, the company's data and analytical services can cater to a wide range of customers, making it resilient against a particular market's slowdown. We rate it a Buy at or below current price.

Analysis

Planet Labs PBC strives to be the "all-seeing" eye on Earth, whose images can be useful for a wide range of purposes. From the ports in LA to wildfires in the UK, from global aircraft monitoring to a capsized destroyer in North Korea, from a tornado destruction in Missouri to a landslide in Switzerland, not to mention the high-altitude methane detection and the solar and wind infrastructure mapping, its Q1 presentation is littered with stunning images of high resolution. High scalability is indeed the core of its investment proposal for its business model. While it performs the daily global scanning on Mother Earth, Planet Labs also recently added hyperspectral imaging to its data, which includes capturing and analyzing the contiguous bands of the electromagnetic spectrum for remote sensing.

These efforts make its product applicable to agriculture, defense & intelligence, civil government, energy, insurance, and natural resources management, among other industries. It shows a diversified revenue mix with 55% from civil government, 25% from defense & intelligence, and 20% from commercial business, spread across all continents. Its largest revenue stream comes from North America at 45%, with 28% and 25% from EMEA and Asia Pacific plus Japan, and 4% from LATAM. It shows overseas revenue is substantial for the company, given the nature of its image, which is global.

Diversification and scalability seem to be working well for Planet Labs. The company's revenue has been growing at an average of 20-30% for the past three years and now is at just below 10%, reaching over $250 million per quarter.

Additionally, it reported a $527 million backlog in the latest quarter, a 140% YoY growth. There is no sign that recent economic uncertainty has posed any disruption to its revenue. On one hand, the scalability and diversification help it to build resilience against a mild slowdown from a particular region or sector; on the other hand, it could spell some Forex risk since more than half of its revenue needs to be converted back to USD.

Planet Labs posted rising free cash flow, even with its CapEx close to tripling compared to where it was in mid-2023. Management called it "a significant milestone for Planet's employees and shareholders." This is because during the same period, it has completely erased its negative $100 million quarterly operating cash flow posted in mid-2023. Its quarterly operating cash flow was about $17.3 million in the latest quarter ending in June 2025 (FY Q1 2026). According to its report, the CapEx spending is mostly on its satellites and R&D infrastructure, including satellite construction and replacement, strengthening the high-resolution and hyperspectral data we alluded to at the beginning, incorporating AI in its proprietary data, plus driving scale with satellite services. In its 10-K, the company stresses that CapEx spending will continue to rise as it "prioritizes customer retention and expansion." That inelasticity means its cash flow generation is the key to its future development and strategy implementation; otherwise, the growth will erode its liquidity instead. This explains why it hails the positive free cash flow as a milestone.

The backbone of Planet Labs' technology is the so-called "Agile Aerospace." The company coined the term "Agile Aerospace", referring to the fast development and deployment of space-related hardware and software in small packages. In such a way, it can send more satellites than its next competitor. This allows it to receive data faster and re-iterate the technological upgrade quicker as well, such as receiving daily data sets, while the traditional data satellite services would have a longer cycle to report data back to Earth. Such a daily data set can grow into a large proprietary database much faster; hence, its proprietary big data and analytics and platform were built on this foundation.

With a solid technological foundation, it is easier to sell more value-added products to customers. Not only has its operating cash flow turned positive, but its earnings have also pulled out of the negative as well. Management credited the "solid increase" to "landing and expanding high-value accounts." Although the customer count has declined, the average revenue per customer has increased.

With such high expenses to maintain its infrastructure, it is natural for Planet Labs to explore new and innovative ways to apply its data and capacity for technological breakthroughs and "market-shaping opportunities." The company naturally has a partnership with NASA, including the Jet Propulsion Laboratory and the NASA Communications Services Project via partnership with Telesat and SES. And some of the analytics companies that use Generative AI and LLMs to produce actionable information from Planet Labs image data are also its partners and go-to-market. Additionally, the company is in an effort to democratize and experiment with its own data by forming education and research programs, etc., to encourage more innovative ways to use its resources. This is an upstream-to-downstream chain to make its technology more agile and widely used—a virtuous cycle. It's worth noting that Planet Labs' gross margin has grown from around 30% in 2022 to now 55%, nearly double in 3 years. Most of its other margins, albeit still negative, have also been improving at a similar pace. It is important for the company to continue to be innovative and supportive of the nascent industry that it is part of, such as making its products accessible to ordinary groups, not just governments or commercial customers. The bigger the pie, the better for Planet Labs. We think it has the right thinking to democratize the product, for no one should be "all seeing" the free world.

Financial Overview and Valuation

A glance at Planet Labs PBC's financials shows one thing stands out to us: the low debt-to-equity ratio at only 4.41%. This is rare for a start-up with a constant need for capital-intensive investment. From a price return point of view to compare the company with its Research and Consulting Services peers, its triple-digit 134.78% appreciation within the past year is enough to make it stand out. These peers, such as LegalZoom (LZ), Acuren Corporation (TIC), ICF International (ICFI), Huron Consulting Group (HURN), and Willdan Group (WLDN), are not in the space industry. Planet Labs's price jump away from these peers was mostly since April this year, after the tariff announcement stirred up geopolitical tensions around the world.

While if we put it against other satellite data and image service providers, such as Spire (SR) and BlackSky Technology (BKSY), then its price/book ratio of 4.4x is at the midrange, while its EV/revenue ratio of 6.9x is at the upper bound, along with BKSY. Spire Global is less profitable than Planet Labs (see our recent analysis on Spire), so its lower valuation is expected in both metrics. And BlackSky's EV/Revenue ratio was valued at over 9x just about a month ago. By comparison, Planet Labs PBC's EV/Revenue ratio, although it also jumped along with BlackSky, has held the ground more steadily. The market seems to have a more rational reaction to Planet Labs. At the end of the day, Planet Labs's revenue is expected to grow much larger at a fast pace. We earlier showed that its revenue is currently growing at about the top single digits on a quarterly basis, which could be about 46% higher in a year if this pace continues. If its earnings could turn solid positive within a year, this valuation isn't too much of a stretch. Interested investors can consider scooping up shares at or below the current level.

For Planet Labs' upcoming earnings release, there are a few things to look for. Planet Labs has been winning major contracts mostly from the European governments, such as an "8-figure ACV expansion contract" from a pilot last year and a "7-figure countrywide contract" with BKG, a German government entity. The European Space Agency has long been a partner that relies heavily on NASA. So continuing to win contracts from Europe seems to be on the cards for the company. This is something we will keep an eye on. Additionally, the downstream civil application from the California Air Resources Board through one of its partners has the chance to revive its falling revenue in the civil government sector. Its core contract with JSAT, which has the potential to expand its satellite services offering that is still in its infancy, is another one to watch. Management said they had been pursuing multiple strategic deals in the making. We will mostly look for the sustained growth of its cash flow and the deal announcements to assess the pace and direction it will grow the remainder of the year. We expect it will beat on both the top line and bottom line again and maintain a positive EBITDA.

Conclusion

It is important for Planet Labs PBC to achieve its positive operating cash flow, given its constant need to spend on CapEx to maintain the high quality of its satellite operation. Given the milestone and its positive momentum in both the top and bottom lines, and the low debt level, the company has the ability to scale up further of its product by experimenting and exploring innovative ideas. We recommend a Buy for interested investors.

data.title: Commodities: Jerome Powell Provides A Boost To Most Markets
data.description:
By Warren Patterson, Ewa Manthey

Energy - India’s secondary tariffs come into effect this week

Oil prices managed to finish last week higher, settling up almost 2.9% higher as enthusiasm over a potential Russia-Ukraine ceasefire wanes. Uncertainty prevails as US President Trump once again threatens to impose tougher sanctions on Russia unless there’s a deal to end the war. Trump said there needs to be more clarity within roughly two weeks. However, the market may be reluctant to read too much into this latest threat, given the lack of action taken by the US administration against Russia following the Trump-Putin summit.

In the near term, we may see the oil market benefit following Fed Chair Jerome Powell’s Jackson Hole speech, which was largely dovish and provided a boost to most risk assets. The market is pricing in more than an 85% probability that the Fed cuts interest rates by 25bp in September, up from around 72% ahead of Powell’s speech.

It's looking increasingly likely that secondary tariffs against India for their purchases of Russian oil will go ahead on August 27. There appears to be little progress in trade talks between India and the US since the US announced the tariff earlier in the month. Furthermore, Indian refiners have been showing increased interest in Russian oil, after state refiners initially paused purchases until there is clarity from the government. If India continues to buy Russian oil despite the 25% secondary tariff, it does little to change the market outlook. Instead, it only confirms the bearish outlook for oil prices.

Speculators continue to reduce their net long in ICE Brent amid a bearish outlook. Speculators sold 23,852 lots over the last reporting week to leave them with a net long of 182,695 lots as of last Tuesday. The move was driven predominantly by longs liquidating. Meanwhile, speculators also sold 19,578 lots in NYMEX WTI, leaving them with a net long of 29,686 lots. This is the smallest position held in WTI since October 2008.

Fading optimism over a Russia-Ukraine peace is providing support for European gas prices. At the same time, concerns over flows to Europe amid upcoming Norwegian maintenance will also provide support to the market. Front-month Title Transfer Facility (TTF) futures managed to settle more than 8% higher over the week. EU gas storage is close to 76% full, below the 91% seen at the same stage last year and lower than the 83% 5-year average.

US natural gas has been more bearish, with Henry Hub down 7.5% over the last week and settling at its lowest level since October 2024. This is despite last week’s storage build coming in below average. However, overall storage remains 5.8% above the 5-year average, while we are moving towards a period where we expect to see reduced cooling demand. This is allowing for larger storage builds ahead of the 2025/2026 winter.

Metals - Gold jumps as Powell signals rate cuts

Gold jumped last Friday as the dollar and bond yields fell after Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell suggested an interest rate cut in September, pointing to increasing labour market risks despite persistent inflation concerns. Traders added to bets the Fed will cut rates next month. With US rate cut bets intensifying after Powell’s speech, gold could be poised for another fresh record high.

World Steel Association data shows that global steel production fell 1.3% year on year to 150.1mt in July, as lower output in China, Japan, Russia, and Germany offset higher output from India and the US. Cumulative global steel output fell 1.9% YoY to 1,086.2mt over the first seven months of the year. Chinese steel production fell 4% YoY for a second straight month to 79.7mt last month amid government efforts to gain control over supply. Over the first seven months of the year, output fell 3.1% YoY to 594.5mt. In the EU, crude steel production dropped 7% YoY to 10.2mt, with Germany (-13.7% YoY) dominating the declines.

data.title: John Hancock Multimanager 2010 Lifetime Portfolio Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Highlights

  • After a tumultuous start to the second quarter, the financial markets regained their footing and provided positive total returns for the full three months.
  • The portfolio, while also delivering a gain, didn't keep pace with its benchmark.
  • Asset allocation and underlying manager results each weighed on relative performance.

Market review and outlook

The world equity markets rebounded from initial weakness to register strong gains in the second quarter. Stocks fell sharply in early April in response to U.S. tariff announcements on concerns that Washington's new approach to trade would lead to slower economic growth. Within a week, however, the U.S. administration reversed course and declared a 90-day pause on tariffs. The move fueled hopes that the initial plan was largely a negotiating tactic, providing investors with confidence that trade policy would ultimately be less restrictive than first thought. In addition, data related to global growth, inflation, and corporate earnings exhibited stability despite the shifting policy backdrop. Equities recovered all of their earlier losses in the ensuing rally, propelling the major world indexes to all-time highs by the end of June. Growth stocks, lowerquality companies, and the mega-cap U.S. technology sector led the way higher, while the value style and defensive investments generally underperformed. This marked a pronounced reversal from the trends in place during the first quarter.

Bonds also delivered positive total returns. After experiencing volatility early in the quarter, the fixed-income markets recovered on the strength of low inflation, moderate growth, and expectations that central banks would continue to ease monetary policy. Credit-oriented market segments performed well in this environment and generally outpaced government debt.

Contributors and detractors

Asset allocation detracted from results, as our diversified approach was out of step with the aggressive rally. The real assets portfolio was the primary detractor due to negative returns for energy stocks and U.S. real estate investment trusts. An overweight position in defensive equities—while positive in absolute terms also weighed on relative performance at a time of heightened investor risk appetites. Allocation in the fixed-income portfolio was a small contributor, primarily as a result of an overweight the emerging markets. The fund's absolute return portfolio detracted. Underlying manager performance also detracted somewhat, with nearly all of the shortfall occurring in the bond portfolio.

Portfolio changes

The first half of the year was characterized by dramatic swings in the markets, both in terms of price movements and investors' rapid shifts among specific asset classes. For our part, we don't attempt to time the market or chase momentum in certain areas that happen to be outperforming in a given month. Instead, we remain focused on the core tenets of our investment process: fundamentals, valuations, and diversification. While we frequently make small changes to the portfolio, the moves are based on rigorous analysis and a three- to five-year outlook. In addition, we strive to keep the portfolio positioned for a broad range of outcomes instead of trying to predict the path of the economy or the direction of government policy. We believe this steady approach, rather than one that attempts to react to the ever-changing daily headlines, is the optimal way to help the fund achieve its longer-term investment objectives.

data.title: BNY Mellon Small/Mid Cap Growth Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
MARKET REVIEW

In 2025, the US and global equity markets are navigating a complex landscape shaped by shifting monetary policies, trade uncertainties and technological disruption.

Equity markets were generally higher across developed and emerging markets as the US retreated on its harshest tariff threats and signs the Federal Reserve (Fed) may begin cutting rates as inflation stabilized. Markets largely looked past geopolitical uncertainty in June as Israel launched surprise airstrikes on Iran’s nuclear sites, prompting retaliatory attacks and US strikes in support of Israel before a fragile ceasefire contained further escalation. The S&P 500 ® Index (SP500)(SPX) increased 10.94% for the quarter. Within the index, 8 of 11 Global Industry Classification Standard (GICS) equity sectors finished in positive territory. The strongest performers were information technology and communication services.

Growth stocks outperformed value stocks, while large-cap outperformed small-cap. Among other major equity benchmarks, the MSCI EAFE Index, a measure of developed markets excluding the US and Canada, was higher by 11.78%. The MSCI Emerging Markets Index was higher by 11.99%. Developed fixed income market performance was positive in the quarter. The Bloomberg® US Aggregate Index returned 1.21%. The Bloomberg Global Aggregate Hedged Index returned 1.61% and the Bloomberg Global Aggregate Unhedged Index returned 4.52%.

In the US, equity market performance was higher in the second quarter of 2025 with the Dow Jones Industrial Average advancing by 5.46% and the Nasdaq 100 advancing by 17.86%. US fixed income markets were also higher for the quarter. First quarter gross domestic product (GDP) contracted by 0.3%, the first decline since 2022, due to a surge in pre-tariff imports. Despite this, the labor market remained resilient, with job growth moderating but consistently above expectations. Inflation appeared contained, with core Personal Consumption Expenditures (PCE), the Fed’s preferred measure, easing from 2.7% to 2.5% in May before rising slightly to 2.8% in June, still below forecasts. The Fed took no further rate actions in the quarter, although Vice Chair Michelle Bowman signaled openness to rate cuts as early as July amid softening job data and subdued inflationary impact from tariffs. Consumer sentiment rebounded from historic lows, while political developments—most notably President Trump’s evolving tariff strategy and the advancement of the “One Big Beautiful Bill”—added fiscal and policy uncertainty heading into the second half of the year.

In developed international markets, equities were broadly higher. In the Eurozone, the European Central Bank (ECB) cut rates by 25 basis points (BPs) twice as inflation continued to moderate. The most recent inflation reading (May) came in at 1.9% relative to 2.2% in April and below the ECB’s long-term target of 2%. First quarter GDP grew at an annualized rate of 1.2%, effectively flat relative to the prior quarter. Despite growth resilience, the European Commission lowered its full-year forecast due to US-EU trade tensions. The UK had a first quarter GDP surge of 0.7%, the fastest in the Group of Seven (G7), which was partly driven by front-loaded activity ahead of new tariffs and tax changes. The Bank of England (BOE) cut rates by 25 bps in May before pausing in June amid lingering inflation concerns. In Japan, inflation spiked early in the quarter due to food and tariff-driven import costs but later moderated to 1.9%. The Bank of Japan (BOJ) left policy unchanged across both its meetings and announced a slower pace of bond purchase tapering beginning in 2026, aiming to stabilize bond market volatility.

Emerging market equities rose during the quarter following a 90-day suspension of the most punitive US tariffs on China and a weaker US dollar. In China, first quarter GDP growth exceeded expectations at 5.4%, though partly driven by front-loaded activity ahead of new US tariffs. The Caixin Manufacturing PMI rebounded into expansion territory in June, supported by improving domestic demand. The People’s Bank of China maintained rates but injected liquidity to support the economy. Brazil posted strong first quarter GDP growth of 2.9% year-over-year, fueled by agriculture, though inflation remained elevated. The central bank raised rates twice, by 50 bps and then 25 bps, before signaling a pause. In Mexico, the economy narrowly avoided recession in quarter one, but the Bank of Mexico cut its growth forecast and continued a dovish stance, reducing rates to 8.0% by quarter-end to counter slowing domestic activity. Meanwhile, India launched a sizable easing cycle, cutting both its repo rate and cash reserve ratio, despite relatively strong growth, to buffer against external trade risks and maintain momentum.

Commodities were lower in the second quarter, with the Refinitiv/CoreCommodity CRB Index returning -2.85%. Crude oil prices were volatile, initially retreating in April and May due to rising Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries (OPEC+) output and global oversupply concerns but ending the quarter higher after spiking in June following the Israel-Iran conflict. Conversely natural gas ended the quarter lower due to milder-than-expected weather cooling demand and steady production. Although gold prices remained resilient in April on safe-haven demand, they declined in May and June as geopolitical risks moderated.

The fund (Class A shares at NAV) outperformed its benchmark, the Russell 2500 Growth Index, during the second quarter of 2025. From a sector perspective, industrials led relative contributors over the period, followed by health care.

Conversely, financials and consumer staples were the most significant laggards.

SECTOR REVIEW

Positive Impacts

Industrials: Industrials led relative contributors over the period. An overweight to the aerospace & defense space particularly benefited returns as Curtiss-Wright Corporation and Karman Holdings were top contributors as both companies reported solid first quarter results.

Health Care: Stock selection among health care also contributed to relative returns, particularly among the biotechnology segment.

Negative Impacts

Financials: Financials led relative detractors by sector over the period. An underweight to the financial services space weighed on results.

Consumer Staples: Consumer staples also detracted from relative returns over the period, primarily due to stock selection among the food products segment. Shares of Freshpet (FRPT) were a top detractor over the period amid a recent deceleration in growth as a result of broader consumer weakness.

STOCK REVIEW1

Key Contributors – Q2 25

Curtiss-Wright Corporation: Curtiss-Wright is an aerospace and naval supplier who has critical technology in nuclear equipment. The stock benefited from positive news flow on nuclear power adoption. Additionally, the company announced strong first-quarter 2025 earnings and a dividend increase.

Vertiv Holdings Co. Class A (VRT): Vertiv is a global leader in power and thermal equipment with approximately 80% sales exposure to artificial intelligence (AI) infrastructure investment. Shares rose over the period after the company reported solid first quarter results with reaccelerated order growth and boosted fiscal year 2025 revenue growth guidance.

Karman Holdings: Karman designs and manufactures subsystems for missile and launch applications including rocket motors, connectors and payload protection equipment. Shares of Karman rose over the period as a result of clean first quarter financial results, showcasing their growth potential and industry-leading profitability, instilling early investor confidence.

Key Detractors – Q2 25

Freshpet: Freshpet is an American pet company that manufactures and markets natural fresh meals and treats for dogs and cats. Freshpet underperformed due to soft scanner data and muted revenue expectations, despite signs of stabilization. Looking ahead, the positive thesis centers on a raised 2027 EBITDA (earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization) margin target, renewed confidence in 25% top-line growth, and Freshpet’s dominant 96% share of the US fresh pet food market. If execution improves and sales reaccelerate, we believe the stock could re-rate meaningfully, especially with valuation support from recent mergers and acquisition comps and expanded retail placement.

Bio-Techne Corporation (TECH): Bio-Techne underperformed in the second quarter of 2025 due to macro policy headwinds, including US executive orders targeting drug pricing that pressured sentiment across the healthcare sector. The company also faced valuation compression amid a market rotation into large-cap AI beneficiaries, leaving more specialized growth names behind. With limited near-term growth visibility, investors grew cautious despite Bio-Techne’s long-term positioning.

Builders FirstSource (BLDR): Builders FirstSource is a leading value-add distributor of building materials to the homebuilder market. Shares fell amid weakness in the US residential construction market.

1. Performance of individual stock evaluated is based on total effect. Total effect represents the opportunity cost of an investment manager's investment decisions relative to an overall benchmark, which can include holdings that are not held by the portfolio but contribute to relative portfolio performance.

MARKET OUTLOOK

In 2025, the US and global equity markets are navigating a complex landscape shaped by shifting monetary policies, trade uncertainties and technological disruption. Interest rates are expected to decline gradually, with the Federal Reserve projected to cut rates by 25 basis points per quarter starting in the third quarter of 2025, potentially lowering the federal funds rate to around 3% by year-end. This easing could support equity valuations by reducing borrowing costs, particularly for small-cap stocks, which are more sensitive to interest rate changes. However, persistent trade policy volatility, driven by tariff negotiations, could elevate inflation risks, potentially tempering the pace of rate cuts. Despite these headwinds, global economic growth is expected to remain resilient, with the US economy growing at 0-1% and avoiding recession, while Europe and emerging markets like India benefit from accommodative policies and domestic growth drivers.

Corporate earnings were poised to be a key driver of equity performance, as US S&P 500 earnings growth had come in at 12.7% in the first quarter, but are now projected to fall to approximately 5% for 2025 according to FactSet. Earnings revisions for US companies have recently trended downward, reflecting caution due to trade uncertainties, while non-US developed markets show more stable earnings outlooks. Despite the “Magnificent Seven” mega-cap tech stocks seeing their earnings growth decelerate, they are still outpacing the broader market of the “remaining” S&P 493 stocks, currently. Internationally, Europe’s forward 12-month earnings growth of over 7% and earnings per share nearing new highs is encouraging, but it appears it may be peaking and turning lower.

Large-cap stocks, particularly in the US, remain favored due to their earnings resilience and exposure to AI-related growth, but small-cap stocks may present compelling opportunities as valuations trade at a sizable discount to fair value. We believe small caps are likely to benefit from lower interest rates and potential deregulation, which could catalyze a rebound, especially in cyclical sectors like industrials and financials. However, their performance hinges on economic stability and tariff resolutions, as they are more exposed to domestic economic fluctuations.

Conversely, large caps, especially tech-heavy names, face risks from elevated valuations and potential AI sector corrections, as seen in early 2025 with the sell-off following DeepSeek’s (DEEPSEEK) disruptive AI model announcement. We think a balanced approach favoring both growth and value stocks across regions could help mitigate risks while capturing upside.

Artificial intelligence continues to be a transformative force, driving productivity gains and reshaping equity market dynamics. The AI sector, while still concentrated in US mega-caps, is broadening as adoption spreads across industries like healthcare and industrials, with Asia (notably Taiwan and India) emerging as potential diversification opportunities. However, concerns about overinvestment in AI infrastructure, highlighted by the market’s reaction to DeepSeek’s cost-effective model, underscore risks of a potential bubble. For 2025, AI’s productivity benefits are expected to bolster corporate margins, but we think we should remain selective, focusing on companies with strong fundamentals and reasonable valuations to navigate volatility. Overall, in our view, a cautiously optimistic outlook prevails, with US equities and selective international markets offering opportunities amidst a volatile macro environment, along with actively managing risks tied to policy uncertainty and AI-driven disruptions.

data.title: John Hancock Infrastructure Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Highlights

  • Global infrastructure stocks recovered from early weakness to post a gain in the second quarter, building on their advance in the first three months of the year.
  • The fund underperformed its broad-based benchmark, the MSCI (MSCI) All Country . World Index.
  • The fund remains focused on producing positive absolute returns over longterm periods, rather than keeping pace with a specific index on a quarter-toquarter basis.

Market review and outlook

As was the case with the equity markets more broadly, infrastructure stocks sold off in early April on worries about the U.S. administration's tariff policies before recovering over the remainder of the quarter. The sector didn't fully participate in the broader rally given investors' strong preference for faster-growing and lowerquality companies. On the other end of the spectrum, more defensive, yieldoriented sectors—such as utilities—lagged. European infrastructure stocks performed particularly well, however, making up for some of the shortfall.

Contributors and detractors

At the industry level, multi-utilities, integrated telecommunication services, and water utilities made the largest contributions to fund performance when both weightings and total returns are taken into account. Oil and gas storage/transportation was the most notable detractor.

The French multinational electric utility Engie (OTCPK:ENGIY) SA, which reported better-thanexpected earnings, was the leading individual contributor to relative performance. The German electric utility E.ON SE further helped results. The company reported robust profits and affirmed its 2025 guidance, putting it on track to achieve the high end of full-year guidance that was already revised higher in February. European utilities continued to capitalize on rising demand for lowcarbon electricity and improvements in regulation and allowed returns, which is expected to encourage more investment and lead to higher growth. E.ON, as the largest operator of electricity distribution networks in Germany, stands to benefit from the country's new fiscal expansion package, which is expected to boost infrastructure spending significantly.

The natural gas transportation company Targa Resources (TRGP) Corp. detracted from performance. The stock was hurt by falling oil prices caused by OPEC+ output hikes and concerns that U.S. trade policy would lead to an economic slowdown. Additionally, management reported first-quarter revenue that was below estimates. Earnings were affected by hostile winter weather, which reduced Permian volumes, as well as higher operating expenses and production costs. ONEOK, Inc. also lagged. The company missed earnings expectations, but it maintained its full-year guidance for 2025, citing confidence in its strategic direction and operational resilience.

Portfolio changes

We initiated a position in SK Telecom Co., Ltd. (SKM), South Korea's largest telecommunications company. We believe the South Korean government's Corporate Value-up Program will improve corporate governance, and we expect that SK Telecom will further enhance shareholder returns to align with its peers and rebuild its reputation following a recent cybersecurity incident. We also added to positions in Italgas S.p.A. (OTCPK:ITGGF), an Italian gas distribution utility and beneficiary of sector consolidation, and Dominion Energy (D), Inc., an integrated U.S. utility that has capitalized on higher power demand from data centers in Virginia. We funded these purchases by selling Public Service Enterprise Group (PEG), Inc., where we no longer saw an attractive risk-and-return profile, and trimming RWE AG (OTCPK:RWEOY).

Multi-utilities and electric utilities were the fund's top sector weightings at the end of June, while its smallest positions were in wireless telecommunication services and independent power producers/energy traders.
